firewall.disclaimer: The domain may be protected with a proprietary or custom WAF which we were unable to identify automatically.

external.keyHint: This check needs a free API key. See README and .env.example for setup.
external.tryLater: Try again in a few minutes

ssl.concept: Comprehensive SSL/TLS evaluation combining certificate analysis, TLS protocol and cipher suite detection, CA trust verification and industry-standard SSL Labs grade.
ssl.purpose: Traffic encryption, MITM protection, forward secrecy (PFS) and certificate renewal tracking — both technical and reputational dimensions.
ssl.tips: Trusted should be Yes, prefer TLS 1.3, ECDHE ciphers provide PFS, target SSL Labs A or A+. Renew if less than 30 days remain.

http-security.concept: Presence, quality and A-F grade of security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, Cross-Origin policies) that restrict browser behavior. Includes HSTS preload status.
http-security.purpose: Browser-level defense against common web attacks like XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, SSL stripping and data leakage — configuration quality at a glance.
http-security.tips: Aim for grade A or B. At minimum CSP, HSTS (max-age ≥ 1 year + includeSubDomains + preload) and X-Content-Type-Options should be present.

firewall.concept: A Web Application Firewall (WAF) inspects traffic at the application layer and filters malicious requests (SQL injection, XSS, bot traffic). Detected via HTTP header fingerprinting.
firewall.purpose: Blocks known attack signatures, provides DDoS protection and adds a layer against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
firewall.tips: Cloudflare, AWS WAF, Sucuri, Fastly, Imperva are common. No WAF isn't always bad but recommended for public services.

security-txt.concept: RFC 9116 standard — the /.well-known/security.txt file contains contact info and policy so security researchers can report vulnerabilities.
security-txt.purpose: Provides a responsible disclosure channel; an indicator of security maturity for serious organizations.
security-txt.tips: Contact, Expires and Policy fields should be present. Expires should not be in the past.

cookies.concept: Analysis of HTTP cookies set by the site and their security flags (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite).
cookies.purpose: Non-HttpOnly cookies can be stolen via XSS; non-Secure ones can leak over HTTP; SameSite=Strict/Lax protects against CSRF attacks.
cookies.tips: Session cookies (session, auth) must have HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite.

dns.concept: Bulk query of all DNS record types (A, AAAA, MX, NS, CNAME, SOA, TXT, SRV), DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) support of nameservers and classification of TXT records by purpose (SPF, DMARC, DKIM, BIMI, verification).
dns.purpose: Where the domain points, how mail is routed, which services are verified and resolver privacy — a holistic view of DNS infrastructure.
dns.tips: Multiple NS records are essential for redundancy, no MX means no mail, DoH-enabled resolvers improve privacy, SPF lookup limit is 10 DNS queries.

dnssec.concept: DNSSEC cryptographically signs DNS responses. DS and DNSKEY records enable validation.
dnssec.purpose: Protects against DNS cache poisoning and spoofing attacks. The resolver verifies the response truly came from the authoritative server.
dnssec.tips: If DNSSEC is enabled, DS record should be in the parent zone, DNSKEY in own zone. Recommended for .gov and .bank domains.

mail-config.concept: Email sender authentication: SPF (which servers can send), DKIM (message signature), DMARC (policy and reporting).
mail-config.purpose: Together they protect against email spoofing and phishing attacks. Missing them causes mail to land in spam.
mail-config.tips: All three are important. DMARC policy should be p=none initially, then quarantine, finally reject.

whois.concept: WHOIS queries domain registration details: who owns it, which registrar, when registered, when it expires.
whois.purpose: Domain age, ownership and duration are trust indicators. Newly registered domains are often used for phishing.
whois.tips: Very near expiration date (e.g. 30 days) is a neglect sign. Post-GDPR, registrant info is often redacted (REDACTED FOR PRIVACY).

subdomains.concept: Certificate Transparency (CT) logs are a public log system where every new SSL certificate is publicly recorded. crt.sh discovers subdomains by querying these logs.
subdomains.purpose: Maps the attack surface — forgotten subdomains like dev/staging/admin are major security risks. Foundational step in OSINT research.
subdomains.tips: Hundreds of subdomains may be spam/auto-generated. Watch for prefixes like dev., staging., admin., test., internal. Wildcard certs appear as a single subdomain.

hostnames.concept: PTR (reverse DNS) records map an IP to a hostname. An IP may have multiple hostnames (shared hosting, CDN).
hostnames.purpose: Used for real IP ownership, shared hosting detection, CDN verification and mail sender reputation.
hostnames.tips: IPs without PTR records are problematic for outbound mail. Many different domains in PTR indicates shared hosting.

location.concept: Resolves all IPv4/IPv6 addresses from A/AAAA DNS records, then finds the primary IP's geographic location, ISP, ASN and position on a map.
location.purpose: Where and which provider hosts the server, CDN usage, privacy (GDPR), latency estimation and traffic patterns — a holistic view of server identity.
location.tips: Multiple IPv4s indicate load balancing/CDN. IPv6 support is a sign of modernity. With CDN, location varies by region.

ports.concept: Attempts to connect to common TCP ports (22-SSH, 25-SMTP, 80-HTTP, 443-HTTPS, 3306-MySQL, etc.) and identifies which are open.
ports.purpose: Unnecessarily exposed services expand the attack surface. Database ports (3306, 5432, 6379) should not be public.
ports.tips: Only 80 and 443 should be open for most web services. If SSH (22) is open, use fail2ban and key-only auth.

trace-route.concept: Shows the network hops (routers) a packet traverses to reach the destination. Measures latency at each hop.
trace-route.purpose: Useful for understanding network topology, finding latency sources, and seeing which countries traffic passes through.
trace-route.tips: High hop count or long delay at a single hop = bottleneck. Hops with asterisks (*) usually hide ICMP behind firewalls.

status.concept: Is the site online, what's the HTTP status code (200, 301, 404, 500, etc.), how fast does it respond — basic health check.
status.purpose: Verifies the site is reachable and healthy. Slow response (>1s) signals a UX problem.
status.tips: 200 = OK, 3xx = redirect, 4xx = client error, 5xx = server error. TTFB (time to first byte) < 200ms is ideal.

quality.concept: Google Lighthouse loads the page in a headless browser and scores it 0-100 on Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices and SEO.
quality.purpose: Provides concrete metrics for user experience, search ranking, conversion rates and accessibility.
quality.tips: 90+ is good, 50-89 average, <50 poor. LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms are Core Web Vitals targets.

carbon.concept: Estimated CO₂ emissions calculated from data transferred during page load, assuming the global energy mix.
carbon.purpose: Web sustainability is a growing concern. Heavy sites are both slow and harmful to the environment; optimization brings technical and ethical benefits.
carbon.tips: Shrink image sizes, remove unnecessary JavaScript, prefer green-energy hosting.

screenshot.concept: Captures a visual screenshot of the page using a headless browser (e.g. Puppeteer).
screenshot.purpose: Useful for seeing how the site looks, detecting defacement (page takeovers) or SSR/CSR rendering differences.
screenshot.tips: Blank or broken image indicates rendering issues. Mobile vs desktop screenshots can be compared.

headers.concept: All HTTP response headers returned by the server — Content-Type, Cache-Control, Server, X-Powered-By, compression info, etc.
headers.purpose: Reveals information about server configuration. Some headers (Server, X-Powered-By) unnecessarily leak stack details.
headers.tips: Hiding Server and X-Powered-By reduces info disclosure. Cache-Control is critical for performance.

tech-stack.concept: Analyzes HTML, header and script signatures to detect frameworks, CMS, CDN, analytics, payment and other technologies used by the site.
tech-stack.purpose: Useful for competitive analysis, security research (detecting old versions), market research and tech benchmarking.
tech-stack.tips: Old versions (WordPress 4.x, old jQuery) may contain known vulnerabilities. Too many 3rd-party scripts are a privacy risk.

social-tags.concept: Open Graph (Facebook, LinkedIn) and Twitter Cards meta tags — the preview image/title/description shown when the page is shared on social media.
social-tags.purpose: Directly affects click-through rates and brand perception. Missing tags result in broken previews.
social-tags.tips: og:title, og:description, og:image (1200x630px) and twitter:card are the minimum required set.

redirects.concept: The chain of 3xx redirects an HTTP request goes through to reach the final URL.
redirects.purpose: Too many redirects weaken SEO, hurt performance, and cycles cause infinite loops. Security-wise can indicate open redirect vulnerabilities.
redirects.tips: Ideal: 0-1 redirect. Common transitions like http→https, www→non-www aside, long chains should be optimized.

robots-txt.concept: /robots.txt is an REP (Robots Exclusion Protocol) standard file telling search engines and crawlers which paths can be crawled.
robots-txt.purpose: For SEO management, hiding admin/private areas from search results, and controlling crawler traffic.
robots-txt.tips: Disallow: / blocks all crawling. Listing sensitive paths in robots.txt does NOT provide security — it just hides from indexing.

sitemap.concept: sitemap.xml is a machine-readable list of all important URLs on the site. It's an indexing guide for search engines.
sitemap.purpose: For faster discovery of new content, priority signals for unimportant pages, and change frequency hints.
sitemap.tips: If URL count exceeds 50,000, a sitemap index file is needed. lastmod dates should be realistic.

linked-pages.concept: Extracts all anchor (a href) links on the page, separated into internal (same domain) and external (different domain).
linked-pages.purpose: Internal linking structure is important for SEO, external links are trust signals but spam links hurt. Useful for crawling depth analysis.
linked-pages.tips: Too many external links can be a spam signal. Low-trust links are marked with rel='nofollow'.

archives.concept: Queries past snapshots of the domain from Wayback Machine (archive.org) and other archives.
archives.purpose: For site history, old versions, removed content and OSINT research. Useful for tracking ownership changes.
archives.tips: First snapshot date is a good indicator of domain age. Few snapshots indicate the domain was rarely visited.

rank.concept: The Tranco list (formerly Alexa) ranks sites by global traffic. Accepted as the standard in academic research.
rank.purpose: A rough indicator of site popularity, competitor comparison and brand awareness.
rank.tips: Sites above 1,000,000 are in the 'tail'. Top 10,000 is considered fairly popular.

block-lists.concept: Privacy-focused DNS resolvers (Cloudflare Family 1.1.1.3, AdGuard, Quad9) and family-protection lists show whether the site is blocked.
block-lists.purpose: To see if the site is flagged as adult/malware/tracking. False flagging affects commercial traffic.
block-lists.tips: Being listed on multiple blocklists is a serious reputation issue. Being on AdGuard is normal (trackers), being on Quad9 is harmful.

threats.concept: Checks whether the domain or host appears on threat intelligence platforms like URLhaus, PhishTank.
threats.purpose: Identifies domains actively hosting malware or running phishing. Browsers use these lists to show warnings.
threats.tips: Not being on these lists is necessary but not sufficient. New attacks may be added to the lists late.

shodan.concept: Shodan.io is a search engine that scans all internet-exposed devices and services. Returns service, banner, port and known vulnerabilities for an IP.
shodan.purpose: For attack surface analysis, exposed databases, old software versions and CVE matches.
shodan.tips: If CVEs appear in vulns, priority update is needed. Unexpected open ports indicate misconfiguration.

malware.concept: Google Safe Browsing API checks URLs against malware, phishing and unwanted software lists. Chrome, Firefox and Safari use these lists.
malware.purpose: To see if the site is blocked by browsers. Being blocked directly hurts advertising and SEO.
malware.tips: Being on Threats lists is an emergency. First clean, then request removal via Search Console.

errors.SSRF_BLOCKED: Target blocked for security reasons (private/internal network).
errors.RATE_LIMITED: Too many requests. Please wait a moment.
